dialogue.utterance: Unit 2, respond for an unconscious patient at 1546 Gehant St.
Hi, I'm Alex. We're here for the unconscious patient - how long has this been going on?
She was found like this lying on the floor in her bedroom, at least 3 to 4 hours ago.
Sir, can you tell me your name so we can confirm she is yours?
Yes, that's my wife, Martha. She's not responding to anything.
She does not open her eyes on her own or upon verbal or painful stimulus.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name? Martha? She's not responding.
She is unresponsive but has slow, weak respirations. Position is left lateral recumbent - let's roll her to supine.
Airway is patent and unobstructed - no signs of active obstruction or stridor.
Respirations are slow and weak, about 18 per minute. No signs of labored breathing.
Pulse is 65 - steady and strong. No gross indications of shock or hypotension.
Applying the pulse oximeter now.
SpO2 is 97% - good oxygenation recorded.
Conducting blood glucose with finger stick.
Blood sugar is 34 - low, indicating possible hypoglycemia.
Measuring blood pressure now. Result is 156 over 62 through the automatic cuff.
Preparing for intravenous access now. Left wrist is our site - let's place a 20G catheter.
Starting 12-lead EKG. Will confirm findings with 4-lead monitoring later.
12-lead results: sinus rhythm with right bundle branch block noted.
Administering 100 ml of Dextrose 10% through the IV now to treat hypoglycemia.
Skin is pale, dry, and intact - no signs of injury or burns.
Head and neck appear unremarkable - no obvious signs of injury or swelling.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth, and throat exam; Medic Partner
dialogue.utterance: Pupils are equal and reactive - about 4 mm bilaterally. No facial droop or swelling. No JVD.
Bilateral breath sounds are clear. No crackles or wheezes. Chest rise is symmetrical and unlabored.
Heart sounds are regular on auscultation.
Abdomen is soft and nontender. Bowel sounds are present and normal.
No signs of vomiting, diarrhea, or urinary urgency - no N/V/D reported.
Pelvis is stable and without deformity - no signs of instability or crepitus.
Extremities show no signs of deformity, angulation, or wounds. No DCAP-BTLS found.
PMS is equal and intact in both upper and lower extremities.
She is unresponsive and showing no signs of pain at this time.
Sir, do you know if she has any medical conditions or uses insulin, perhaps in the past?
I think she might be diabetic, but I'm not sure. No medications I know of.
When was the last time she ate or had fluids?
I don't know exactly - but it's been several hours.
What were her activities right before you found her on the floor?
She was in the bedroom - alone. I came back and found her collapsed.
Was there any vomiting, fever, or confusion before she collapsed?
No - she just fell and didn't wake up since.
Rechecking blood sugar for response. What's the result now?
Glucose has improved to 217 - positive response to the dextrose.
SpO2 is 98, stable.
Heart rate has decreased to 62 - consistent with improved glucose.
Respirations remain at 16 per minute - steady and non-labored.
Blood pressure is 172 over 93 - still within safe range for now.
Setting up 4-lead monitoring now - still confirms sinus rhythm.
No new findings on the EKG - monitoring will continue during transport.
We are under hypoglycemia protocol 3-8. Continue IV monitoring and glucose checks.
Vital signs are stable so far, no signs of deterioration.
Is there a history of allergies, like to medications or food?
None I'm aware of. She has no known allergies she's mentioned to me.
Has she consumed alcohol lately - might that have contributed?
I don't think so - not her usual behavior. That doesn't seem likely.
When she was like this for hours, was it the same day today?
Yes, maybe around noon-I wasn't with her the whole time.
No signs of trauma to the head or neck - eyes, ears, and nose are intact.
No known medical history - she is unresponsive but improving with treatment.
No reported urinary symptoms - no evidence of dribbling or urgency.
No signs of major bleeding - skin findings remain pale and dry.
Reinitiating cardiac monitoring - no changes from the baseline ekg.
Blood sugar remains stable at 217. No further dextrose needed.
Heart rate is 62 - showing sustained positive response to interventions.
No increased pain observed - GCS is improving per en route reassessments.
Mentioned any prior episodes or preexisting conditions?
She doesn't recall. I wasn't with her the entire time, just cooking.
Patient is being taken to [CITY] at your request, Mr. - okay?
Yes, that's fine - I want to stay with her.
Patient is securely fastened with straps on the stretcher. Medical team is on standby.
Keep her in slight head-up position - vitals are stable.
I can ride along - let me try to stay calm.
That's fine. Please don't move suddenly and just hold her hand near her chest.
Patient loaded onto stretcher - systems are on, vitals are capturing.
Still normal sinus rhythm - no signs of cardiac compromise from initial readings.
IV line remains in place and functioning well.
She is now opening her eyes to verbal stimuli - AOX1.
Martha, what year is this?
I think it's either 2019 or 2018.
It's 2020, Martha. You were found unresponsive in your home earlier.
Rhythms are still stable, EKG unchanged from prior.
Vitals continue to show progress. She's in an early cognitive state.
Continue to recheck blood glucose in short intervals and note results.
About five minutes to the hospital - she remains slightly confused.
Time to transition from the ambulance stretcher to the hospital gurney.
GCS is now 12, and her cognition is improving - but she remains disoriented.
IV is secure and continuing, vitals are steady.
Hospital team, arriving now at the front entrance - [COMPANY_NAME], call 1546 Gehant St.
She is on file here - proceed to the entrance.
Her SpO2 is still 98% - no signs of hypoxia.
Heart rate is still 62 - overall stable.
Respiratory rate is 16 per minute - stable.
Recorded as 172 over 93 - still trending stable.
EKG rhythm remains unchanged at sinus rhythm.
She is appropriately dressed and secured for handoff.
Thank you. We're ready at the bed - sign in red.
All patient data and records have been transferred to the ER.
ED, we are at the threshold - bed split is needed.
En route back - all actions completed in line with protocol.
All documentation and video logging is complete and reflects our actions accurately.
Call is complete - had a minor delay finding the correct home but responded well.
All signs pre-hospital are captured in the data - ongoing observation needed.
GCS improved to 12, verbalization only her own name.
All actions per ePCR are documented and assigned to the correct timestamps.
